title: On-chain privacy and chain analysis
summary: Address reuse, coinjoin, payjoin, silent payments, what blockchain surveillance firms can and can't see, taproot's privacy implications. Practical and philosophical takes on financial privacy.

edges.label: Address reuse and the default privacy floor
edges.summary: Why receiving twice to the same address ties your transactions together, and how HD wallets are designed to make a fresh address the easy path.

edges.label: Coinjoin
edges.summary: Equal-output coinjoins (JoinMarket, the WabiSabi family) break the one-to-one input/output mapping a chain analyst relies on. What they actually achieve and what they don't.

edges.label: Payjoin (BIP78)
edges.summary: A pay-to-merchant transaction where the recipient secretly contributes an input. The output amounts no longer add up to a single sender's coins, breaking the common-input-ownership heuristic.

edges.label: Silent payments (BIP352)
edges.summary: A static address you can publish anywhere that produces a unique on-chain output for every payment, with no extra interactivity and no on-chain notification.

edges.label: Taproot's privacy implications
edges.summary: Why taproot makes single-sig, multisig, and complex script spends look identical on chain, and the limits of that guarantee.

edges.label: What chain analysis firms actually do
edges.summary: The clustering heuristics, the exchange-subpoena pipeline, and the gap between marketing claims and what's structurally provable from the chain alone.
